```mermaid
    gate1 --- radA["Radical platforms originate here"]
    gate6 --- disA["Disruption realized post launch"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between invention and innovation with a suitable biotechnology example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Invention | Innovation |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Creation of a novel idea, device, or process | Successful commercialization of that invention |
| Market linkage | Not required | Mandatory |
| Risk | Scientific only | Scientific + commercial + regulatory |
| Example | First demonstration of CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing in a petri dish (2012) | Commercial launch of Casgevy, the first CRISPR-based therapy, by Vertex/CRISPR Therapeutics (2023) |

Invention is the **seed**; innovation is the **fruit** that reaches the market.

(3 marks: 1 for distinction, 1 for example, 1 for clarity of expression.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any four types of innovation and give one biotechnology example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Incremental Innovation** – Annual reformulation of the seasonal influenza vaccine to match circulating strains.
2. **Radical Innovation** – Development of mRNA vaccine platform technology by BioNTech/Moderna during COVID-19.
3. **Open Innovation** – AstraZeneca's open-source sharing of its COVID-19 vaccine sequence for non-profit global distribution.
4. **Frugal Innovation** – Low-cost, paper-based diagnostic strips (e.g., for sickle cell disease) designed for rural clinics.

(3 marks: 0.5 per correct type, 0.25 per correct example — total 3.)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Explain the **Henderson–Clark model** of innovation in detail. Construct the 2×2 matrix and define all four quadrants with biotechnology examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Henderson–Clark model (1990) classifies innovation along **two dimensions** of knowledge: *component knowledge* and *architectural knowledge*. When a firm introduces a product/process change, it may alter either or both.

**2×2 Matrix (2 marks for correct construction):**

|  | Architecture **Unchanged** | Architecture **Changed** |
|---|---|---|
| **Component Changed** | Modular Innovation | Radical Innovation |
| **Component Unchanged** | Incremental Innovation | Architectural Innovation |

**Definitions with biotech examples (5 marks – 1.25 per quadrant):**

1. **Incremental Innovation** – Refinement of an existing technology without changing components or architecture. *Example:* Annual strain update of the seasonal influenza vaccine; new formulation strength of insulin glargine.

2. **Modular Innovation** – A core component is replaced while the overall architecture stays the same. *Example:* Switching the expression host from *E. coli* to *Pichia pastoris* in recombinant protein production while keeping the same downstream purification train.

3. **Architectural Innovation** – Components remain the same, but they are reconfigured into a new architecture. *Example:* Repurposing an existing fermentation plant (same bioreactors) to manufacture a new enzyme product by re-engineering the process flow, sensors, and control loops.

4. **Radical Innovation** – Both the components and their architecture change. *Example:* The mRNA vaccine platform introduced by BioNTech/Moderna, which replaced both the biological component (mRNA vs. inactivated virus) and the architectural delivery system (lipid nanoparticles vs. viral capsid).

**(b)** A South Korean biotech startup **CelluraBio** has built its entire business on a proprietary *ex vivo* CAR-T cell therapy for B-cell lymphomas. Recently, a competitor in China announces an off-the-shelf allogeneic CAR-T product priced at **30% of CelluraBio's autologous therapy**. Discuss the **type of innovation** the Chinese firm represents using **Christensen's framework** and explain the **strategic threat** to CelluraBio. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Identification using Christensen's framework (3 marks):**

- The Chinese product is **allogeneic (off-the-shelf)** — meaning it serves patients who **cannot** use autologous (patient-derived) therapies, such as those with rapidly progressing disease who cannot wait 4–6 weeks for personalized manufacturing.
- This opens a **new patient segment** that the autologous modality historically could not serve.
- The product is also **priced 30% lower**, making it competitive on cost.
- Therefore, the Chinese firm represents **New-Market Disruptive Innovation** (with a strong **Low-end disruption** secondary dimension).

**Strategic threat to CelluraBio (4 marks):**

1. **Customer migration:** Hematologists managing rapidly progressing lymphoma cases will prefer the allogeneic option, eroding CelluraBio's high-acuity patient base.
2. **Margin compression:** Even within the existing autologous segment, payers will demand price concessions, eroding CelluraBio's premium pricing power.
3. **Cost-of-goods disadvantage:** Autologous manufacturing is intrinsically expensive (one batch = one patient); CelluraBio's COGS cannot match the economies of scale of an allogeneic platform.
4. **Innovation response options for CelluraBio:**
   - Pivot to a hybrid platform (autologous for niche indications, out-license allogeneic rights).
   - Invest in **architectural innovation** to automate autologous manufacturing and reduce COGS.
   - Pursue **incremental innovation** by expanding into earlier lines of therapy where autologous is still preferred.
   - Defensive M&A to acquire an allogeneic platform.

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]*

**(a)** Discuss the concept of **Disruptive Innovation** as proposed by Clayton Christensen. Differentiate between **low-end disruption** and **new-market disruption** with one biotechnology example each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Christensen's Disruptive Innovation Theory (2 marks):**

Clayton Christensen (Harvard Business School, 1997) defined disruptive innovations as those that **create a new market and value network**, eventually disrupting an existing market and displacing established market leaders. Disruption is not about being better; it is about being **different in a way that the market values**, often at lower cost or with greater accessibility.

**Low-end disruption (2.5 marks):**
- Targets **over-served customers** at the bottom of an existing market with a "good enough" product at a **lower price**.
- The incumbent retreats upmarket; the disruptor captures the mass market.
- *Biotech example:* **Biosimilars** — generic versions of biologic drugs (e.g., infliximab biosimilars) undercutting Remicade at 50–70% lower cost, capturing price-sensitive hospital formularies.

**New-market disruption (2.5 marks):**
- Targets **non-consumers** who were previously outside the market because the existing offering was too expensive, too complex, or inaccessible.
- *Biotech example:* **23andMe's direct-to-consumer genetic testing kits** (\$99–\$199) made personal genomics accessible to ordinary consumers who would never have visited a clinical geneticist, thereby creating a brand-new consumer genomics market.

---

**(b)** A Kerala-based agri-biotech startup **GreenGene Agritech** develops a low-cost, solar-powered, AI-integrated soil microbiome analysis device targeted at smallholder farmers in Wayanad. The device is designed in collaboration with ICRISAT (an external research body) and uses freely available open-source firmware. The company sells the device at **one-fifth the price** of imported soil-test kits. Identify and justify the **multiple types of innovation** represented by GreenGene Agritech. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

GreenGene Agritech represents a **convergence of at least five innovation types**. Each must be identified with explicit justification (1.4 marks each, rounded to 7 total).

1. **Frugal Innovation (1.4 marks):** The device is explicitly designed for **affordability** ("one-fifth the price") and **resource constraint** (solar-powered, suitable for off-grid rural Wayanad). This is the textbook definition of frugal innovation, focused on cost reduction without sacrificing core functionality.

2. **Open Innovation (1.4 marks):** Collaboration with **ICRISAT** (external research body) and the use of **open-source firmware** exemplifies the open-innovation paradigm, where a firm leverages external knowledge sources to accelerate and de-risk internal R&D.

3. **Reverse Innovation (1.4 marks):** The product is designed **first for an emerging-market rural context** (Kerala's smallholder farmers) rather than being a downscaled version of a developed-market product. It is purpose-built for the underserved base of the pyramid.

4. **Business Model Innovation (1.4 marks):** The pricing strategy (one-fifth of imports) combined with the device-as-a-service potential (micro-leasing to farmer cooperatives) represents a fundamentally different revenue logic compared to the capex-heavy model of imported soil-test kits.

5. **Sustainable/Green Innovation (1.4 marks):** The **solar-powered** operation and the focus on **soil microbiome health** align with regenerative agriculture, positioning this as a sustainability-positive innovation contributing to environmental and social goals.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "types" with "stages"** — Discovery, Development, and Commercialization are *stages* of innovation, NOT *types*. Examiners routinely deduct 2–3 marks when students list these.
> 2. **Henderson–Clark is a 2×2 matrix, not a 2×2 list** — always draw the matrix and label the axes explicitly. Students who merely define the four types without showing the matrix lose **2 marks**.
> 3. **Disruption ≠ any new technology** — many students label any radical innovation as "disruptive." Disruption is specifically a **market-and-business-model** phenomenon, not purely a technological one. Use Christensen's definition strictly.
> 4. **Open Innovation is not the same as Open Source** — open innovation is a *business strategy*; open source is a *licensing model*. GreenGene uses both, but they are distinct concepts.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Innovation ≠ Invention.** Invention is the idea; innovation is the **commercialized deployment**.
- The **Henderson–Clark 2×2 matrix** classifies innovation into **Incremental, Modular, Architectural, and Radical** based on whether *component knowledge* and/or *architectural knowledge* change.
- **Christensen's disruptive innovation** has two variants: **low-end** (price-undercutting of over-served customers) and **new-market** (creation of a market of non-consumers).
- The **expanded typology** includes **Process, Service, Business Model, Marketing, Open, User, Reverse, Frugal, and Green/Sustainable** innovation.
- For a biotech entrepreneur, the **innovation type dictates capital intensity, risk, time-to-market, IP strategy, and team structure.**
- Always apply the **5-step identification algorithm** (Trigger → Henderson–Clark Test → Disruption Test → Source/Sustainability Filter → Final Classification) when solving case-based exam questions.
- Common exam pitfalls: confusing stages with types; misusing the term "disruptive"; failing to draw the Henderson–Clark matrix.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creativity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT835 - ENTREPRENEURSHIP DEVELOPMENT IN BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Creativity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CREATIVITY (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Creativity is the **cognitive process** of generating novel, useful, and valuable ideas, solutions, products, or processes through the recombination of existing knowledge, perspectives, and resources. In the context of **Biotechnology Entrepreneurship**, creativity refers to the ability of a bio-entrepreneur to identify unmet market needs, invent novel bio-products (drugs, enzymes, diagnostics, transgenic crops), and design innovative business models that translate scientific discoveries into commercially viable enterprises.

The term was popularized in psychology by **Joy Paul Guilford (1950)**, who distinguished between *convergent* (single correct answer) and *divergent* (multiple novel answers) thinking. Modern entrepreneurial scholarship (Schumpeter, Drucker, Csikszentmihalyi) defines creativity as the **engine of innovation**—the "creative destruction" that fuels new venture creation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "DNA of Ideas"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Biotech Kitchen Chef 🍳**
> Think of a biotechnology entrepreneur as a **master chef in a molecular kitchen**. The pantry (knowledge base) contains ingredients—amino acids, genes, enzymes, patient data, market insights. A non-creative cook simply follows an old recipe (existing drugs, conventional therapies). A **creative chef**, however, recombines unusual ingredients—say, an antibody fragment with a nanoparticle delivery system—to invent an entirely new dish (a targeted cancer therapy). Creativity, therefore, is **NOT inventing from nothing**; it is the **artful recombination of what already exists** into something that has never existed before. Just as PCR recombines DNA strands, creativity recombines mental concepts.

## 1.3 Core Characteristics of Creativity

A creative output (whether an idea, product, or venture) typically exhibits **five signature traits**:

| # | Characteristic | KTU-Friendly Meaning | Biotech Example |
|---|----------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Novelty** (Newness) | Has never existed before in this form | First mRNA vaccine platform (BioNTech, Moderna) |
| 2 | **Usefulness** (Value) | Solves a real problem or creates economic worth | CRISPR-Cas9 gene-editing tool |
| 3 | **Relevance** (Context-fit) | Matches the needs of a specific market gap | Affordable COVID-19 diagnostic kit for rural India |
| 4 | **Surprise** (Non-obviousness) | Connects ideas that were previously considered unrelated | Spider-silk protein produced in goat milk (Nexia Bio) |
| 5 | **Synthesizability** (Actionable) | Can be transformed from idea to prototype | Penicillin mass-production via fermentation (Florey, Chain) |

## 1.4 Creativity vs. Innovation vs. Invention

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Distinguish Carefully (Common Exam Trap!)**
> - **Creativity** = Generation of the *idea* (the "spark"). It is a **mental act**.
> - **Invention** = The first *technical realization* of that idea as a tangible artifact or process (the "blueprint made real").
> - **Innovation** = The *commercialization* and diffusion of that invention into the marketplace (the "market scale-up").
>
> **Equation of the Thought Process:**
>
> $$\text{Creativity} \longrightarrow \text{Invention} \longrightarrow \text{Innovation} \longrightarrow \text{Entrepreneurship}$$
>
> A biotechnology entrepreneur must be capable of executing **all four links** in this chain.

## 1.5 The KTU Cognitive Framework for Creativity

KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes that creativity is not a mystical trait but a **trainable cognitive skill** governed by three pillars:

1. **Knowledge Base (Domain Expertise)** — Deep familiarity with molecular biology, bioinformatics, regulatory pathways, market needs.
2. **Creative Thinking Skills (Mental Processes)** — Fluency, flexibility, originality, elaboration, sensitivity (Guilford's divergent-thinking abilities).
3. **Motivation & Task Commitment** — Intrinsic curiosity and persistence (the "10-year overnight success" syndrome in pharma R&D).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Perspective:** When a question asks *"List the components of creativity,"* writing these **three pillars** scores full marks. Adding an introductory sentence about Guilford or Csikszentmihalyi earns a bonus half-mark for academic depth.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Matrix

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundations of Creativity

### 2.1.1 Psychometric View — Joy Paul Guilford (1950, 1967)

Guilford, in his **Structure of Intellect (SOI) Model**, separated human intellect into:
- **Convergent Thinking** → Narrowing down to the *one correct* answer (analytical, logical).
- **Divergent Thinking** → Expanding outward to generate *many possible* answers (creative).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Guilford's Four Divergent-Thinking Abilities (MUST memorize for 3-mark questions):**
>
> $$\text{Creative Output} = f(\text{Fluency}, \text{Flexibility}, \text{Originality}, \text{Elaboration})$$
>
> - **Fluency** = Number of ideas produced (quantity).
> - **Flexibility** = Number of *categories* of ideas (variety).
> - **Originality** = Statistical rarity of the idea (uniqueness).
> - **Elaboration** = Level of detail and decoration on the chosen idea.

### 2.1.2 Componential View — Teresa Amabile (1983, 1996)

Amabile's **Componential Theory of Creativity** asserts that creativity at any moment is the intersection of three components:

$$
\text{Creativity}_{\text{in task}} = f(\text{Expertise}, \text{Creative-thinking skills}, \text{Motivation})
$$

- **Expertise** = Domain-relevant knowledge (e.g., knowing recombinant DNA technology).
- **Creative-thinking skills** = Cognitive style, application of heuristics, problem-solving style.
- **Task motivation** = Intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivation. **Intrinsic motivation boosts creativity; heavy extrinsic rewards can sometimes *undermine* it** (the *overjustification effect*).

### 2.1.3 Systems View — Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi (1996)

Creativity emerges from the interaction of three systems, **not** from the individual alone:

$$\text{Creativity} = \{\text{Individual} \cap \text{Domain} \cap \text{Field}\}$$

- **Individual** = The creative person with genetic predisposition + personality + life experience.
- **Domain** = The symbolic rule-system of the discipline (e.g., the laws of molecular biology).
- **Field** = The gatekeepers (peers, journals, VCs, regulators) who validate and propagate the new idea.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biotech Application:** A scientist publishing in *Nature* (field gatekeeping) about a novel protein-folding prediction (domain knowledge) becomes creative only when the *individual's* unique angle intersects both. Without the field accepting it, the work remains an idea, not a creative contribution.

### 2.1.4 Economic View — Joseph Schumpeter (1934, 1942)

Schumpeter coined **"Creative Destruction"** (*schöpferische Zerstörung*), describing how entrepreneurs do not merely find new combinations—they *dismantle* old industries while building new ones.

$$
\text{Entrepreneurial Function} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{New Combination}_i)
$$

His famous **five types of new combinations (Neue Kombinationen)** are mandatory for KTU exams:

| # | Type of New Combination | Biotech Illustration |
|---|------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **New product** | A CAR-T cell therapy for leukemia |
| 2 | **New method of production** | Continuous-flow bioreactor instead of batch fermentation |
| 3 | **New market** | Selling point-of-care malaria tests in Africa |
| 4 | **New source of supply** | Plant-based (molecular farming) production of vaccines in tobacco |
| 5 | **New organization of industry** | A contract research organization (CRO) for clinical trials |

## 2.2 The Creative Process — Wallas' Four-Stage Model (1926)

Graham Wallas, in *The Art of Thought*, proposed the most cited process model of creativity. KTU examiners LOVE this.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wallas' Four Stages of Creativity (Mandatory for 14-mark answers):**
>
> 1. **Preparation** — Conscious gathering of information, defining the problem, intense study. *(Example: a biotech founder spends 2 years researching unaddressed rare diseases.)*
> 2. **Incubation** — Subconscious processing where the mind mulls over the problem without active effort. The "aha" is brewing. *(Example: long walks, vacations, dreams.)*
> 3. **Illumination** — The "Eureka!" moment; the sudden flash of insight. *(Example: Katalin Karikó's decade-long conviction about mRNA during skepticism.)*
> 4. **Verification** — Logical evaluation, experimentation, refining, scaling. *(Example: clinical trials, regulatory submission, market testing.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Trap:** Do **NOT** write "Inspiration" instead of "Illumination." Wallas used the term *Illumination* (illuminated idea). Using *Inspiration* costs 1 mark in valuation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Creativity Frameworks

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Rapid Revision)**

| Framework / Theory | Key Equation or Core Principle | Author / Year | Application in Biotech |
|--------------------|--------------------------------|---------------|------------------------|
| Divergent Thinking | $\text{Creativity} = \text{Fluency} + \text{Flexibility} + \text{Originality} + \text{Elaboration}$ | Guilford, 1950 | R&D brainstorming sessions |
| Componential Theory | $\text{C} = \text{Expertise} \times \text{Creative-Skills} \times \text{Motivation}$ | Amabile, 1983 | Hiring & team composition |
| Systems Model | $\text{C} = \text{Individual} \times \text{Domain} \times \text{Field}$ | Csikszentmihalyi, 1996 | IP & publication strategy |
| Creative Destruction | $\Delta\text{Market} = \sum (\text{New Combinations})$ | Schumpeter, 1942 | Startup pitch decks |
| Creative Process | $\text{Prep} \rightarrow \text{Incub} \rightarrow \text{Illum} \rightarrow \text{Verif}$ | Wallas, 1926 | Lab notebook entries |
| Osborn's CPS | $\text{Process} = \text{Mess-Find} \rightarrow \text{Data-Find} \rightarrow \text{Problem-Find} \rightarrow \text{Idea-Find} \rightarrow \text{Solution-Find} \rightarrow \text{Acceptance-Find}$ | Osborn, 1953 | Diagnostic kit development |
| Knowledge Threshold | $\text{If Knowledge} < \text{Threshold} \Rightarrow \text{No Creativity}$ | Sternberg, 1999 | Why deep biology knowledge is mandatory |
| Five-Step Innovation | $\text{Knowledge Absorption} \rightarrow \text{Assimilation} \rightarrow \text{Transformation} \rightarrow \text{Exploitation}$ | Drucker, 1985 | Spin-off creation from universities |

## 2.4 Creative Problem Solving (CPS) — Osborn-Parnes Model (1953, 1967)

Alex Osborn (the *O* in BBDO advertising) developed a **structured problem-solving methodology**. KTU often asks this as a 7-mark sub-question.

**The Six Phases:**

1. **Mess-Finding (Objective Finding)** — Acknowledge the broad, fuzzy challenge.
2. **Data-Finding (Fact-Finding)** — Gather facts; clarify the *knowns*.
3. **Problem-Finding** — Define the *exact* problem statement.
4. **Idea-Finding** — Generate many solutions (brainstorming).
5. **Solution-Finding (Idea Evaluation)** — Screen, refine, develop ideas.
6. **Acceptance-Finding (Implementation)** — Plan execution, secure buy-in.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biotech Application:** When a startup wants to address *antimicrobial resistance*, Mess-Finding might be "Save the world from superbugs." After Data-Finding, Problem-Finding narrows it to: *"Develop a topical bacteriophage gel for diabetic foot ulcers at < ₹500/dose."* This precision is what creativity-with-discipline delivers.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Creativity in Biotechnology Entrepreneurship

The biotechnology sector is uniquely creativity-dependent because of its long gestation, capital intensity, and biological uncertainty. Creativity manifests in:

- **Scientific creativity** → Novel molecular targets (e.g., RNA interference, 2010 onwards).
- **Technological creativity** → New bioprocess designs (single-use bioreactors).
- **Business-model creativity** → Subscription-based drug access models (e.g., Novartis's Zolgensma lottery, later changed to outcome-based pricing).
- **Marketing creativity** → Direct-to-consumer genetic testing (23andMe).
- **Regulatory creativity** → Adaptive trial designs for orphan drugs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters for KTU Examiners:** A 14-mark question on creativity often expects you to **link creativity → bio-entrepreneurial opportunity → commercial outcome**. Merely defining creativity without linking it to a *biotech-specific* context loses 3-4 marks in valuation.

## 2.6 Barriers to Creativity (KTU Hot Question)

> [!WARNING]
> **Frequently tested as "Discuss the barriers to creativity faced by bio-entrepreneurs." Memorize at least 6.**

1. **Functional fixedness** — Seeing only the conventional use of a tool (e.g., using PCR only for DNA amplification, not for point-of-care diagnostics).
2. **Perceptual blocks** — Inability to view a problem from a new angle.
3. **Cultural blocks** — Taboos against genetic engineering in certain societies.
4. **Emotional blocks** — Fear of failure, risk-aversion common in capital-intensive pharma.
5. **Intellectual blocks** — Lack of cross-disciplinary knowledge (biology + AI + ethics).
6. **Environmental blocks** — Hostile university policies for faculty entrepreneurship.
7. **Resource scarcity** — Lack of lab equipment, seed capital, mentorship.

## 2.7 Techniques for Stimulating Creativity (Highly Scorable!)

For a 14-mark question, including this block guarantees full marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seven Creativity-Stimulating Techniques (Must remember):**
> 1. **Brainstorming** (Osborn, 1953) — Defer judgment, quantity over quality, build on others' ideas.
> 2. **Reverse Brainstorming** — *"How could we make this problem worse?"* then invert.
> 3. **SCAMPER** (Eberle, 1971) — Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to other uses, Eliminate, Rearrange.
> 4. **Lateral Thinking** (de Bono, 1967) — Non-linear, pattern-breaking thinking; deliberately leaving the obvious path.
> 5. **Mind Mapping** (Buzan, 1974) — Visual branching from a central concept.
> 6. **Morphological Analysis / Attribute Listing** — Systematically varying parameters of a system.
> 7. **Analogical Thinking** — Borrowing solutions from one domain (e.g., mimicking shark-skin texture for antibacterial hospital surfaces).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Conceptual Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation of the Componential Creativity Equation

The KTU 2024 Scheme asks students to **explain how creativity emerges from underlying components**. Let us derive Amabile's componential model step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Define the output
The output of any creative act at moment $t$ is the creative product (idea, prototype, formulation).

$$
C(t) = \text{Creative Product at time } t
$$

### Step 2 — Identify the three influencing variables

According to Amabile, three classes of variables influence the magnitude of $C(t)$:

- $E(t)$ = Expertise level (domain-relevant facts, technical skills, special domain talents).
- $C_s(t)$ = Creative-thinking skills (cognitive style, heuristic usage, work style).
- $M(t)$ = Task motivation (intrinsic motivation baseline + extrinsic motivators − extrinsic inhibitors).

### Step 3 — Set up the functional relationship

Empirically, expertise and creative skills combine **multiplicatively** because a person with low expertise can rarely produce creative work *even* with high motivation (a "knowledge threshold" effect; Sternberg, 1999). Therefore:

$$
C(t) = E(t) \times C_s(t) \times M(t)
$$

### Step 4 — Inject the overjustification correction

Amabile's later work (1996) showed that extrinsic motivators can *crowd out* intrinsic motivation under certain conditions. We model this by separating motivation:

$$
M(t) = M_{\text{intrinsic}}(t) - \alpha \cdot M_{\text{extrinsic}}(t) + \beta \cdot M_{\text{synergistic}}(t)
$$

where $\alpha$ is the overjustification penalty coefficient ($\alpha \in [0.2, 0.5]$ typically) and $\beta$ is the synergy coefficient for aligning extrinsic rewards with intrinsic passion.

### Step 5 — Final consolidated equation

$$
\boxed{C(t) = E(t) \times C_s(t) \times \big[M_{\text{intrinsic}}(t) - \alpha \cdot M_{\text{extrinsic}}(t) + \beta \cdot M_{\text{synergistic}}(t)\big]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight for Students:** This equation is *conceptual* (not numerically calibrated) and explains **why biotech founders like Karikó and Jennifer Doudna succeeded** — they had extremely high $M_{\text{intrinsic}}$ despite low $M_{\text{extrinsic}}$ for years.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Creativity Frameworks to Biotech Startup Lifecycle

| Stage of Biotech Startup | Dominant Creativity Type | Most Useful Framework | Key Deliverable |
|--------------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| **Ideation** (Months 1-6) | Scientific + Problem-finding | Wallas' Preparation + Osborn's Problem-Finding | Patentable hypothesis |
| **Proof-of-Concept** (Months 6-24) | Technological creativity | Componential Theory (build expertise) | Working prototype |
| **Pre-Seed Funding** (Months 12-18) | Business-model creativity | Schumpeter's New Combinations | Investor pitch deck |
| **Scale-up** (Year 2-5) | Process & Manufacturing creativity | Amabile's Motivation pillar | GMP-certified process |
| **Market Launch** (Year 5-10) | Marketing & Access creativity | Drucker's Innovation Principles | Distribution model |
| **Maturity / Pivot** (Year 8+) | Strategic renewal | Csikszentmihalyi's Field-gatekeeping | New therapeutic indication |

## 3.3 Worked Example — Applying SCAMPER to a Failing Biotech Product

**Context:** A student team has developed a slow, expensive **lateral-flow antigen test kit** for dengue. The product is losing to cheaper competitors. Apply **SCAMPER** to revive it.

> [!NOTE]
> **SCAMPER Step-by-Step Application (model answer format for 14-mark questions):**

| Letter | Action | Question | Creative Result for Dengue Kit |
|--------|--------|----------|-------------------------------|
| **S** | Substitute | What materials/ingredients can be substituted? | Replace gold-nanoparticle label with **upconversion nanophosphors** (longer fluorescence, fewer false-positives). |
| **C** | Combine | What can be merged with the kit? | Combine with a **smartphone AI reader** that photographs and quantifies the band intensity. |
| **A** | Adapt | What can be adapted from another field? | Adapt **pregnancy-test strip architecture** (already FDA-approved design) for dengue antigen capture. |
| **M** | Modify / Magnify / Minify | What can be made bigger or smaller? | **Minify** strip size to 2 cm and **magnify** antibody conjugation density for higher sensitivity. |
| **P** | Put to other uses | Who else could use this? | **Veterinary clinics** for canine dengue surveillance — entirely new market. |
| **E** | Eliminate | What can be removed? | Eliminate the cassette plastic housing; use **paper-microfluidic origami**. |
| **R** | Rearrange / Reverse | What if the order of steps changed? | **Reverse** sample flow direction using capillary action so that blood flows *backwards* onto a pre-immobilized conjugate pad. |

**Final Creative Output:** A smartphone-coupled, paper-microfluidic dengue test kit, costing < ₹40, deployable in veterinary and human clinics, and patentable in three jurisdictions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is a model 14-mark answer:** It demonstrates structured creativity, lists all seven SCAMPER letters, and shows *biotech-specific application*, not a generic toothpaste example.

## 3.4 Quantitative Creativity Assessment — Guilford's Scoring

Although rare in KTU exams, if a numerical question appears:

**Torrance Test of Creative Thinking (TTCT, 1966, 1990)** scores creativity using four normalized metrics:

$$
\text{TTCT Creativity Index} = 0.30 \cdot \text{Fluency} + 0.30 \cdot \text{Originality} + 0.20 \cdot \text{Flexibility} + 0.20 \cdot \text{Elaboration}
$$

Each component is scored against age-normed percentiles. A TTCT score in the **90th percentile or above** indicates *highly creative* individuals — useful for hiring R&D scientists.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Reasoning: Why Creativity Matters in Biotech but Not in Commodity Industries

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Chain (Use this in 14-mark "Discuss" questions):**

1. **Premise 1:** Biotechnology is a *knowledge-intensive, science-driven* sector.
2. **Premise 2:** Knowledge-intensive sectors have *rapid obsolescence* (Moore's Law equivalent in sequencing: cost per genome fell from \$100M in 2001 to \$200 in 2024).
3. **Premise 3:** Firms that fail to creatively renew face *creative destruction* by newcomers (Schumpeter).
4. **Premise 4:** Hence, creativity is the **primary competitive moat** in biotech, unlike commodity manufacturing where economies of scale dominate.
5. **Conclusion:** Therefore, bio-entrepreneurs must institutionalize creativity through R&D processes, hiring profiles, and innovation-friendly governance.

## 3.6 Concept Derivations Frequently Asked

### 3.6.1 Knowledge Threshold Theory (Sternberg & Lubart, 1999)

$$
\text{If } K_{\text{domain}} < K_{\text{threshold}}, \text{ then } C \to 0
$$

In other words, **below a minimum knowledge level, creativity is mathematically impossible**, regardless of motivation. This is why an arts graduate cannot meaningfully invent a new antibiotic molecule.

### 3.6.2 Intrinsic Motivation Principle (Amabile, Deci & Ryan)

$$
M_{\text{intrinsic}} \propto \text{Autonomy} \times \text{Competence} \times \text{Relatedness}
$$

This is the **Self-Determination Theory (SDT)** equation. It explains why bio-entrepreneurs thrive when given **autonomous research freedom** (e.g., academic spinoffs) but struggle under heavy extrinsic monitoring.

### 3.6.3 Creative Destruction as Economic Displacement

$$
\text{Market Value}_{t+1} = \text{Market Value}_t - \text{Destroyed}_{\text{obsolete}} + \text{Created}_{\text{novel}}
$$

The net is positive in the long run (Schumpeterian growth), explaining why *venture capital* finances creative destruction even at short-run industry losses.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Part A — 3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Scheme]**
> *"Define creativity. Differentiate it from innovation with suitable examples from biotechnology."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Creativity is the ability to generate novel and useful ideas by recombining existing knowledge, perspectives, and resources. It is a *cognitive process* of idea generation.
- **Difference Table (2 marks):**

| Basis | Creativity | Innovation |
|-------|------------|------------|
| Nature | Mental/idea-generation | Commercialization-oriented |
| Outcome | A concept or hypothesis | A market-launched product |
| Risk | Low (idea-stage) | High (market-stage) |
| Example | Concept of mRNA-based vaccines | Actual rollout of Moderna and Pfizer COVID-19 vaccines |

**Biotech Example:** The *idea* of using CRISPR-Cas9 for gene editing is creativity; the *commercial launch* of CRISPR Therapeutics' CTX001 (exa-cel) for sickle cell disease is innovation.

---

### Question 2 (Part A — 3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**
> *"List the four stages of Wallas' creative process model and write one sentence on each."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per stage sentence + 0.5 for sequence):**

1. **Preparation** — Conscious effort to define, study, and gather information about the problem.
2. **Incubation** — Subconscious mulling-over where the mind processes the problem without active effort.
3. **Illumination** — The sudden "Eureka" flash where the idea emerges into conscious awareness.
4. **Verification** — Logical testing, experimentation, and refinement of the idea to confirm its value.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (Part B — 14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1 Internal Choice Set A]**
> *(a)* Explain Amabile's **Componential Theory of Creativity**. Discuss how its three components apply to building a creative R&D culture in a biotechnology startup. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* Describe the **seven creativity-stimulating techniques** (Brainstorming, Reverse Brainstorming, SCAMPER, Lateral Thinking, Mind Mapping, Morphological Analysis, and Analogical Thinking). Apply any two to a hypothetical **low-cost point-of-care dengue diagnostic** challenge. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question A — Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Amabile's Componential Theory (3 marks):**
According to Teresa Amabile (1983, 1996), creativity emerges from the multiplicative interaction of three components:

1. **Expertise** — Domain-relevant factual knowledge, technical skills, and special talents. In biotech, this means deep familiarity with molecular biology, bioinformatics, regulatory frameworks, and market needs.
2. **Creative-thinking skills** — Cognitive style, heuristic usage, and a flexible work style that allows breaking conventional patterns.
3. **Task motivation** — Especially *intrinsic* motivation (passion, curiosity), supplemented by appropriate extrinsic motivators.

The conceptual equation:

$$
C(t) = E(t) \times C_s(t) \times M(t)
$$

**Why multiplicative?** Amabile showed empirically that creativity is zero if any component is absent. For example, an unmotivated expert produces routine work; a passionate novice lacks the technical depth.

**Application to a biotech startup (4 marks):**

- **Build Expertise:** Hire PhD scientists with domain depth; partner with academic institutes; conduct weekly journal clubs.
- **Cultivate Creative-thinking:** Run monthly hackathons, interdisciplinary journal clubs (biology + data science + regulatory).
- **Drive Intrinsic Motivation:** Offer equity, mission-driven narrative, autonomy in research direction; minimize micromanagement.
- **Avoid Overjustification:** Do **not** tie all rewards to immediate milestones; celebrate exploratory research too.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Seven Creativity-Stimulating Techniques (3.5 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Brainstorming** — Free-flowing group idea generation without judgment.
2. **Reverse Brainstorming** — Asking "How could we make this worse?" and inverting the answers.
3. **SCAMPER** — Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to other uses, Eliminate, Rearrange.
4. **Lateral Thinking** — Non-linear, deliberate pattern-breaking (Edward de Bono).
5. **Mind Mapping** — Visual radial diagramming from a central concept (Tony Buzan).
6. **Morphological Analysis / Attribute Listing** — Systematic variation of system parameters.
7. **Analogical Thinking** — Borrowing solutions from unrelated domains.

**Application 1 — SCAMPER on low-cost dengue POC diagnostic (2 marks):**

| Letter | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| S (Substitute) | Substitute gold nanoparticles with **latex beads** to lower cost. |
| C (Combine) | Combine antigen-detection with **smartphone AI reader** for quantification. |
| A (Adapt) | Adapt **pregnancy-test cassette** design (already FDA-approved). |
| M (Modify) | Modify strip geometry for **capillary-flow enhancement**. |
| P (Put to other uses) | Use the test for **canine dengue surveillance** (new market). |
| E (Eliminate) | Eliminate plastic housing, switch to **paper microfluidics**. |
| R (Rearrange) | Reverse sample flow to use **gravity-driven delivery**. |

**Application 2 — Analogical Thinking on dengue diagnostic (1.5 marks):**
Borrow the principle of **pH-paper colorimetry** (chemistry) to design a paper-based dengue strip where intensity of color (depth of NS1 antigen binding) is read by a smartphone camera. Analogies between pH detection and antigen detection enable cheaper designs.

---

### Question B (Part B — 14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1 Internal Choice Set B]**
> *(a)* What is **"Creative Destruction"**? Explain Joseph Schumpeter's **five types of new combinations**. Provide one biotech example for each combination. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* Discuss the **barriers to creativity** faced by bio-entrepreneurs in India. Suggest **five measures** to overcome them. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Creative Destruction (2 marks):**
Coined by Joseph Schumpeter in *Capitalism, Socialism and Democracy* (1942), "creative destruction" describes the process by which innovation displaces existing products, services, and even entire industries. Entrepreneurs are the agents of this disruption, creating new value combinations that obsolete old ones.

**Schumpeter's Five New Combinations (5 marks — 1 each):**

| # | New Combination | Biotech Example |
|---|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **New Product** | A **CAR-T cell therapy** product like Kymriah (Novartis) for pediatric leukemia |
| 2 | **New Method of Production** | **Continuous perfusion bioreactors** instead of fed-batch cultures for monoclonal antibody production |
| 3 | **New Market** | **Direct-to-consumer DNA ancestry testing** sold online by 23andMe in India |
| 4 | **New Source of Supply** | **Molecular farming** of vaccines in tobacco plants (Medicago's COVID vaccine) |
| 5 | **New Organization of Industry** | **Contract Development & Manufacturing Organizations (CDMOs)** like Syngene, Biocon, that disrupted the in-house R&D model |

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Barriers to Creativity in Indian Bio-Entrepreneurship (3.5 marks):**

1. **Capital intensity & funding scarcity** — Lab equipment, clinical trials, and regulatory compliance are expensive; venture capital is concentrated in metros.
2. **Regulatory complexity** — Multiple approvals (RCGM, GEAC, DCGI, FSSAI for food-biotech) create a "permission-grant" wall.
3. **Cultural risk-aversion** — Indian academic culture often penalizes failure, deterring bold experimentation.
4. **Brain-drain & weak industry-academia linkage** — Top researchers migrate abroad; universities discourage faculty start-ups.
5. **Infrastructure gaps** — Limited shared facilities, biotech incubators in Tier-2/3 cities.
6. **IP-awareness deficit** — Researchers often lack training in patent writing and freedom-to-operate analysis.
7. **Talent gap in cross-disciplinary skills** — Biology graduates often lack coding, regulatory, and business training.

**Five Measures to Overcome (3.5 marks):**

1. **Strengthen biotech incubators** — BIRAC's BioNEST scheme should be expanded; shared infrastructure for prototype development.
2. **Industry-academia partnerships** — Mandatory internships, dual-degree programs, sabbaticals for industry exposure.
3. **Regulatory single-window** — One digital platform for all approvals (on the lines of the *Single Window Clearance System*).
4. **Reward innovation & failure-tolerant funding** — Government grants modeled on SBIR (US) with non-dilutive funding for early-stage ideas.
5. **Curriculum overhaul** — Embed creativity tools (SCAMPER, Design Thinking, CPS) into B.Tech biotech programs; mandatory entrepreneurship courses.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in the "Creativity" Module:**
> 1. **Confusing Creativity with Innovation** — costs 1–2 marks immediately. Always define *creativity as the idea*, *innovation as the commercialized outcome*.
> 2. **Writing generic examples** (e.g., "Steve Jobs invented the iPhone") — examiners want **biotech-specific** examples (mRNA vaccines, CRISPR, monoclonal antibodies).
> 3. **Listing SCAMPER letters without examples** — naming the technique without showing its *application* loses 1 mark per letter.
> 4. **Wrong Wallas stage names** — calling *Illumination* as "Inspiration" or *Verification* as "Evaluation" is a partial-mark trap.
> 5. **Omitting Indian context** — KTU is a Kerala university; examiners reward *Kerala/India-specific* examples (e.g., Kochi-based startups like *Vellon Space* or *Eyestem Research*).
> 6. **Ignoring the feedback loop in Wallas' model** — Creativity is iterative, not linear. Diagrams must show the cyclic arrow.
> 7. **Writing only definitions without frameworks** — KTU's NEP 2020 outcomes demand application of frameworks (CPS, SCAMPER, Amabile), not mere definitions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Recap of "Creativity" — Module 1 (Print & Pin):**

- [ ] **Definition:** Creativity = generation of *novel + useful* ideas by recombining existing knowledge.
- [ ] **Creativity vs. Invention vs. Innovation:** Idea → Realization → Commercialization → Entrepreneurship.
- [ ] **Five Characteristics:** Novelty, Usefulness, Relevance, Surprise, Synthesizability.
- [ ] **Guilford's Divergent Thinking:** Fluency + Flexibility + Originality + Elaboration.
- [ ] **Amabile's Componential Theory:** $C = E \times C_s \times M$ (multiplicative!).
- [ ] **Csikszentmihalyi's Systems Model:** Individual × Domain × Field.
- [ ] **Schumpeter's Creative Destruction:** Five new combinations — new product, new production method, new market, new supply source, new organization.
- [ ] **Wallas' Four Stages:** Preparation → Incubation → Illumination → Verification (cyclic, not linear!).
- [ ] **Osborn-Parnes CPS:** Mess-Find → Data-Find → Problem-Find → Idea-Find → Solution-Find → Acceptance-Find.
- [ ] **SCAMPER:** Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to other uses, Eliminate, Rearrange.
- [ ] **Other techniques:** Brainstorming, Reverse Brainstorming, Lateral Thinking (de Bono), Mind Mapping (Buzan), Morphological Analysis, Analogical Thinking.
- [ ] **Knowledge Threshold (Sternberg):** Without minimum domain knowledge, creativity collapses to zero.
- [ ] **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan):** Intrinsic motivation depends on Autonomy × Competence × Relatedness.
- [ ] **Barriers:** Functional fixedness, perceptual/emotional blocks, cultural taboos, environmental constraints, resource scarcity, IP-awareness deficit.
- [ ] **Biotech-Specific Examples to Memorize:**
   - mRNA vaccines → creativity of Karikó
   - CRISPR-Cas9 → creativity of Doudna & Charpentier
   - mAb production in CHO cells → process creativity
   - Molecular farming in tobacco → supply-source creativity
   - Affordability innovations (e.g., TrueNat TB test) → market-creativity
- [ ] **Overjustification Effect:** Heavy extrinsic rewards can erode intrinsic motivation — important for designing R&D incentive systems.
- [ ] **Three Pillars of Fostering Creativity:** Knowledge base + Creative-thinking skills + Motivation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Concept of Design Thinking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT835 - ENTREPRENEURSHIP DEVELOPMENT IN BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Concept of Design Thinking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Design Thinking** is a *human-centered, iterative, and experimental problem-solving methodology* that integrates the analytical rigor of engineering, the creative intuition of design, and the empirical evidence of social sciences to develop innovative solutions that are simultaneously **Desirable** to users, **Feasible** to engineer, and **Viable** for business sustainability.

In the context of **Biotechnology Entrepreneurship**, Design Thinking provides a structured framework to translate unmet clinical, agricultural, environmental, or industrial needs into commercially viable bio-based products, processes, and services. The concept was popularized by **Tim Brown** (CEO of IDEO) and refined at the **Stanford d.school (Hasso Plattner Institute of Design)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard Wording):**
> "Design Thinking is a non-linear, iterative process that uses empathy, experimentation, and critical analysis to balance what people *desire*, what is *technically feasible*, and what is *strategically viable* — enabling entrepreneurs to navigate from problem definition to market-ready biotechnological innovation."

## 1.2 The Three Lenses of Innovation (The Core Equation)

The operational foundation of Design Thinking rests on the intersection of three lenses:

$$ \text{Innovation} = f(\text{Desirability}, \text{Feasibility}, \text{Viability}) $$

| **Lens** | **Question Answered** | **Biotech Entrepreneurship Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Desirability** (Human-Centered) | *Do people actually want it?* | Do diabetic patients prefer an oral insulin patch over injections? |
| **Feasibility** (Technology-Centered) | *Can we actually build it?* | Can we stabilize insulin at room temperature using nano-encapsulation? |
| **Viability** (Business-Centered) | *Can we sustain it financially?* | Can we price the patch affordably while maintaining a 35% gross margin? |

> [!NOTE]
> A brilliant biotechnological invention that lacks **Desirability** will fail in the market. A desired innovation that is not **Feasible** stays in the lab. A feasible, desired idea without **Viability** is a hobby — not a business. **Design Thinking harmonizes all three.**

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: "The Master Chef vs. The Cook"**
> A *cook* follows a fixed recipe — if one ingredient is missing, they stop. A *Master Chef* first **observes** the customer (Empathize), **identifies** why they are dissatisfied with the menu (Define), **brainstorms** novel flavor combinations (Ideate), **prepares** a small tasting portion (Prototype), and **serves** it to gather feedback before the full production (Test). If feedback is poor, they iterate — they do not serve the dish blindly.
> 
> In biotechnology, the *cook* is a researcher who builds a product and searches for a problem. The *Master Chef (Design Thinker)* is an entrepreneur who first understands a real human need and then engineers the biotechnological solution to satisfy it. This is the **fundamental shift** KTU wants you to internalize.

## 1.4 The Five Stages — A Snapshot

The Stanford d.school canonical model consists of **five iterative stages**:

1. **Empathize** — Understand the user's needs, emotions, and pain points.
2. **Define** — Synthesize empathy findings into a clear *Point of View (POV)* or problem statement.
3. **Ideate** — Generate a wide range of creative, unconventional solutions.
4. **Prototype** — Build quick, low-cost, tangible versions of selected ideas.
5. **Test** — Solicit user feedback on prototypes and iterate back to earlier stages.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** Design Thinking is *not* a linear 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 pipeline. It is a **non-linear, iterative loop**. You may return to *Empathize* after *Test*, or jump to *Prototype* directly from *Ideate*. Examiners specifically test this iterative understanding — stating it is purely linear will cost you marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 Stage-by-Stage Deconstruction

### **Stage 1: Empathize (The Discovery Phase)**

- **Why it exists:** Most biotech failures occur because innovators *assume* user needs instead of *observing* them. Empathy removes assumption bias.
- **How it works:** Researchers conduct *user interviews*, *contextual inquiries* (e.g., visiting hospitals, farms, fermentation plants), and *immersion exercises*.
- **Key tools:** Stakeholder mapping, empathy maps (Says / Thinks / Does / Feels), patient journey mapping, ethnographic observation.
- **Biotech-specific application:** A med-tech entrepreneur building a *point-of-care dengue diagnostic* spends time in primary health centers in rural Kerala, observing nurses, patients, and ASHA workers.

### **Stage 2: Define (The Problem-Framing Phase)**

- **Why it exists:** A poorly framed problem guarantees a poorly designed solution.
- **How it works:** All empathy data is clustered using tools like the *Affinity Diagram*. The team writes a *Point of View (POV) statement*:

$$ \text{POV} = \text{[User]} + \text{[Need]} + \text{[Insight]} $$

- **Example:** *"A rural health worker (User) needs a rapid, electricity-free dengue detection method (Need) because current ELISA kits require 6 hours and lab infrastructure (Insight)."*
- **Output:** A *How-Might-We (HMW) question*, e.g., *"How might we design a paper-strip dengue test that delivers results in under 15 minutes without electricity?"*

### **Stage 3: Ideate (The Creativity Phase)**

- **Why it exists:** To break free from conventional, often incremental, thinking.
- **How it works:** Structured *brainstorming* using rules: defer judgment, encourage wild ideas, build on others' ideas, focus on quantity.
- **Key techniques:** SCAMPER, mind-mapping, Worst Possible Idea (reverses psychological blocks), 6 Thinking Hats (de Bono).
- **Biotech-specific application:** Generating 50+ approaches to a dengue test — including CRISPR-based lateral flow, smartphone spectrophotometry, and synthetic biology biosensors.

### **Stage 4: Prototype (The Learning Phase)**

- **Why it exists:** Ideas are cheap; prototypes are the *cheapest way to fail fast and learn fast*.
- **How it works:** Build rough, throwaway, minimum-viable representations — they can be physical models, role-played service journeys, or even hand-drawn mockups.
- **Key principle:** *Prototyping is thinking with your hands.* The goal is *learning*, not *perfection*.
- **Biotech-specific application:** A *bench-scale mini prototype* of a lateral flow assay using spiked serum samples before full GLP validation.

### **Stage 5: Test (The Validation Phase)**

- **Why it exists:** To validate (or invalidate) assumptions with real users in real contexts.
- **How it works:** Users interact with prototypes; feedback is captured; the team *iterates* — frequently returning to **Empathize** or **Define** when deeper issues surface.
- **Biotech-specific application:** Field-testing the prototype in 3-5 primary health centers, measuring sensitivity, specificity, time-to-result, and user satisfaction.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Framework Cheat Sheet

| **Design Thinking Element** | **Description** | **Biotech Entrepreneurship Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **Empathy Maps** | Says / Thinks / Does / Feels quadrants | Map the daily reality of a Type-1 diabetic adolescent |
| **POV Statement** | User + Need + Insight | Define the unmet need of a lab technician in a low-resource setting |
| **HMW Question** | "How might we ___?" | Convert the POV into a creative design challenge |
| **Prototype** | Low-fidelity, fast, disposable model | 3D-printed mockup of a portable bioreactor |
| **Three Lenses Check** | Desirability + Feasibility + Viability | Validate every prototype iteration against all three |
| **Iteration Loop** | Non-linear, return to earlier stages | Refine prototype based on field-test feedback |
| **5 Stages** | Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test | Apply in sequence but iterate freely between stages |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Biotechnology & Engineering

Design Thinking is the **operating system of modern biotech startups**. It is formally embedded in:

- **Stanford d.school** curriculum (origin institution)
- **IDEO** consulting methodology (Tim Brown, "Change by Design", 2009)
- **IBM Enterprise Design Thinking** framework
- **MIT BioInnovate** (medical device innovation using design thinking)
- **KTU's own OEBBT835 syllabus** (Module 1 foundations)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** For a biotechnology graduate, Design Thinking bridges the gap between *bench science* and *marketplace reality*. It is the same framework used by founders at **Roche, Biocon, Theranos-disciplines, and CRISPR Therapeutics** when translating laboratory discoveries into patient-impacting products.

## 2.4 The Design Thinking Mindset (Tim Brown's Seven Tenets)

According to **Tim Brown (2009)**, Design Thinking is supported by a specific mindset:

1. **Empathy** — Understand people at a deep emotional level.
2. **Integrative Thinking** — Hold two opposing ideas simultaneously to find a better third idea.
3. **Optimism** — Believe that a better solution is always possible.
4. **Experimentalism** — Try, fail, learn, and try again.
5. **Collaboration** — Work in multidisciplinary teams (scientists + designers + business + users).
6. **Reframing** — Look at the problem from a completely new angle.
7. **Bias toward Action** — Think by doing, not just by talking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Process Walk-Through & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Design Thinking Iterative Equation

While Design Thinking is qualitative, its operational logic can be expressed formally. Let the innovation outcome $I$ be a function of three independent, equally weighted lenses:

$$ I = D \cdot F \cdot V $$

Where:
- $D$ = Desirability score (user validation, $0 \leq D \leq 1$)
- $F$ = Feasibility score (technical capability, $0 \leq F \leq 1$)
- $V$ = Viability score (commercial sustainability, $0 \leq V \leq 1$)

For innovation to occur, the threshold condition is:

$$ I \geq I_{\text{threshold}} = 0.5 $$

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Evaluation Logic

**Step 1 — Empathy Mapping produces D:**
Conduct 20+ user interviews; quantify pain-point severity. A score $D \geq 0.6$ indicates strong user demand.

**Step 2 — Feasibility (F) is computed as:**

$$ F = \frac{T_{\text{achievable}}}{T_{\text{required}}} \cdot R_{\text{resource}} $$

Where $T$ represents technical readiness levels (TRL 1-9) and $R_{\text{resource}}$ is the resource availability index (0 to 1).

**Step 3 — Viability (V) is computed as:**

$$ V = \frac{\text{Net Present Value (NPV)}}{\text{Total Investment}} \cdot M_{\text{market}} $$

Where $M_{\text{market}}$ represents the addressable market size factor.

**Step 4 — Iteration Rule:**

If $I < I_{\text{threshold}}$ after a stage, the entrepreneur **iterates back** to the earliest failing stage:

$$ \text{If } D < 0.6 \rightarrow \text{return to Empathize} $$

$$ \text{If } D \geq 0.6 \text{ but } F < 0.5 \rightarrow \text{return to Ideate} $$

$$ \text{If } D \geq 0.6, \, F \geq 0.5 \text{ but } V < 0.5 \rightarrow \text{return to Define} $$

## 3.2 Worked Biopharmaceutical Example: "Oral Insulin Patch"

A KTU biotech student team wants to develop an *oral insulin patch* for Type-1 diabetic patients in semi-urban Kerala.

### Stage 1 — Empathize
- Interview **15 diabetic patients** at Government Medical College, Thrissur.
- Empathy map reveals: Patients *say* "I will manage," but *feel* "constant needle anxiety" and *think* "I wish there was a pill."
- $D = 0.85$ (very high desirability).

### Stage 2 — Define
- **POV:** A Type-1 diabetic in Kerala *needs* a needle-free insulin delivery method *because* injection-site bruising and social stigma reduce treatment adherence.
- **HMW:** *"How might we deliver insulin non-invasively through a once-daily oral patch?"*

### Stage 3 — Ideate
- Brainstorm 40 ideas: buccal film, transdermal microneedle patch, nano-encapsulated enteric tablet, inhaled aerosol, etc.
- Selected: *pH-responsive buccal mucoadhesive patch with insulin-loaded chitosan nanoparticles.*

### Stage 4 — Prototype
- Build **5 rough prototypes** using a 3D-printed patch mold and lab-synthesized chitosan nanoparticles.
- Cost: ₹2,000 per prototype (vs. ₹5 lakh for full GMP batch).

### Stage 5 — Test
- *In vitro* release studies show 70% insulin release over 6 hours.
- Test on 3 healthy volunteers for safety and adhesion; iterate to improve mucoadhesion.

### Validation Calculation

$$ D = 0.85, \quad F = 0.65, \quad V = 0.55 $$

$$ I = 0.85 \times 0.65 \times 0.55 = 0.304 $$

$$ I = 0.304 < I_{\text{threshold}} = 0.5 $$

$$ \therefore \text{Return to Define stage to reframe the business model.} $$

The team pivots to a **subscription-based direct-to-patient model**, improving $V$ to 0.78:

$$ I_{\text{new}} = 0.85 \times 0.65 \times 0.78 = 0.431 $$

Still below threshold — they iterate **Ideate** with a simpler, cheaper formulation, raising $F$ to 0.85:

$$ I_{\text{final}} = 0.85 \times 0.85 \times 0.78 = 0.564 \geq 0.5 \quad \checkmark $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight:** The above numerical walk-through demonstrates the **iterative loop**. KTU frequently asks students to *apply* the framework to a biotech scenario. Memorizing the 5 stages alone earns only 3-4 marks; demonstrating *iteration logic* and *three-lens validation* can earn full 14 marks.

## 3.3 Design Thinking vs. Traditional Problem-Solving — Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Traditional Problem-Solving** | **Design Thinking** |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting Point** | Solution-first ("We have a CRISPR platform, what can we sell?") | Problem-first ("What unmet clinical need can we solve?") |
| **User Role** | Passive recipient of the solution | Active co-creator and validator |
| **Process** | Linear, sequential | Iterative, non-linear, looping |
| **Failure Handling** | Failure to be avoided | Failure as a learning mechanism (fail fast) |
| **Knowledge Source** | Domain expertise, literature | Empathy + domain expertise + user insights |
| **Output** | Optimized existing solution | Novel, user-validated, viable innovation |
| **Risk Profile** | High late-stage risk (failure at launch) | Low late-stage risk (failure caught at prototype) |
| **Team Structure** | Single-discipline | Multidisciplinary (biologist + engineer + designer + business) |
| **Metrics of Success** | Technical performance specs | User adoption + commercial success + social impact |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Design Thinking Iterative Loop (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STAGE 1: EMPATHIZE\nObserve and engage users\nTools: Interviews, Ethnography, Empathy Maps"] --> nodeB["STAGE 2: DEFINE\nFrame the problem as POV and HMW\nTools: Affinity Diagram, POV Statement"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["STAGE 3: IDEATE\nGenerate diverse creative solutions\nTools: Brainstorming, SCAMPER, Mind Maps"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["STAGE 4: PROTOTYPE\nBuild quick low fidelity models\nTools: Mockups, 3D Prints, Bench Scale Models"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["STAGE 5: TEST\nValidate with real users\nTools: Field Tests, User Feedback, Iteration"]
    nodeE -- "New insights surface" --> nodeA
    nodeE -- "Problem reframing needed" --> nodeB
    nodeE -- "Better solution required" --> nodeC
    nodeD -- "Prototype invalid" --> nodeC
    style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeB fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeD fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CORE["INNOVATION SWEET SPOT"]
        nodeCenter["DESIRABLE\nFEASIBLE\nVIABLE\nBIOTECH INNOVATION"]
    end
    nodeD2["DESIRABILITY\nDo users want it?\nLens: Human Centered"]:::dStyle
    nodeF2["FEASIBILITY\nCan we build it?\nLens: Technology Centered"]:::fStyle
    nodeV2["VIABILITY\nShould we build it?\nLens: Business Centered"]:::vStyle
    nodeD2 --- nodeCenter
    nodeF2 --- nodeCenter
    nodeV2 --- nodeCenter
    classDef dStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```

## 4.3 Design Thinking vs. Traditional R&D (Comparative Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRAD["TRADITIONAL BIOTECH R and D APPROACH"]
        tA["Step 1: Lab Discovery"] --> tB["Step 2: IP Filing"]
        tB --> tC["Step 3: Search for Application"]
        tC --> tD["Step 4: Market Launch"]
        tD -- "Low adoption risk" --> tFail["FAILURE LATE IN CYCLE"]
    end

        dB --> dC["Step 3: Ideate Solutions"]
        dC --> dD["Step 4: Rapid Prototype"]
        dD --> dE["Step 5: Test and Iterate"]
        dE -- "Continuous loop" --> dA
        dE --> dF["VALIDATED MARKET FIT"]
    end
    style tFail fill:#FF6347,color:#fff,stroke:#333
```
