# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Trademark. Differentiate between ™ and ® symbols. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

A **trademark** is a legally recognizable distinctive sign — such as a word, logo, symbol, design, or combination thereof — that identifies and distinguishes the **goods or services** of one enterprise from those of another, as defined under **Section 2(zb) of the Trade Marks Act, 1999**.

The two key symbols are:

| Symbol | Name | Legal Status |
|---|---|---|
| **™** | Trademark | Unregistered common law right — usable freely without registration |
| **®** | Registered | Legally enforceable only after official registration under Section 23 |

> [!NOTE]
> In India, using **® without registration is a punishable offence** under Section 107 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999, with imprisonment up to 3 years or a fine up to ₹2 lakhs, or both.

### Question 2: What is the Nice Classification? Why is it important for biotech trademark filing? [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Nice Classification** is an international system established by the **Nice Agreement (1957)** that divides all goods and services into **45 distinct classes** — Classes 1–34 for goods and Classes 35–45 for services. Each trademark application must specify the class(es) under which protection is sought.

**Importance for Biotech Filings:**
- **Class 5** — Pharmaceuticals, biologics, and diagnostic preparations
- **Class 10** — Medical devices, surgical instruments, diagnostic apparatus
- **Class 1** — Chemicals, enzymes, and biological preparations for industrial use
- **Class 42** — Biotechnology R&D services
- **Class 44** — Medical and agricultural services

It ensures that trademark rights are **scoped narrowly** to specific commercial activities, preventing overreach and conflicts across unrelated industries.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A: Comprehensive Answer**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **procedure for registration of a trademark in India** under the Trade Marks Act, 1999. Mention the statutory time limits at each stage. [7 Marks]

**(b)** Explain the **types of trademarks** recognized under Indian law with suitable biotech industry examples. [7 Marks]

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) Trademark Registration Procedure in India**

The procedure is governed by the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** and the **Trade Marks Rules, 2017**:

1. **Trademark Search:** A preliminary search is conducted on the IP India public database (https://ipindiaonline.gov.in) to check for identical or similar existing marks.

2. **Application Filing:** The applicant files **Form TM-A** either:
   - **Online** at the IP India e-filing portal (recommended)
   - **Offline** at the appropriate Trademark Registry office (Mumbai, Delhi, Kolkata, Chennai, Ahmedabad)

   **Fee Structure (2024):**
   - Individuals/Startups/Small Enterprises: **₹4,500 per class** (e-filing)
   - Others: **₹9,000 per class** (e-filing)

3. **Acknowledgment & Allotment:** The application number is allotted, and the filing date is recorded.

4. **Vienna Codification:** For logo/figurative marks, the Registrar assigns a Vienna Classification code (e.g., 01.15.23 = "Trees with branches").

5. **Examination:** The Trademark Examiner evaluates the application against:
   - **Absolute Grounds of Refusal (Section 9)** — Marks devoid of distinctive character, descriptive, generic, or contrary to public order/morality.
   - **Relative Grounds of Refusal (Section 11)** — Conflict with prior identical/similar marks.

6. **Examination Report:** Issued within **3–6 months** of filing. The applicant must respond within **30 days** (extendable by another 30 days) to overcome objections.

7. **Publication in the Trademark Journal:** If accepted, the mark is published in the weekly Trademark Journal for public notice.

8. **Opposition Period (4 Months):** Any third party can file a **Notice of Opposition (Form TM-O)** under **Section 21** within **4 months** of publication.

9. **Registration:** If no opposition is filed, or if the opposition is decided in the applicant's favour, the Registrar issues the **Registration Certificate** under **Section 23**.

10. **Validity:** The registration is valid for **10 years** from the date of application and can be renewed indefinitely under **Section 25** (renewal fees: ₹9,000 for online filing per class).

**(b) Types of Trademarks Recognized in India**

| Type | Definition | Biotech Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Word Mark** | Plain text brand name | "Humira®" (adalimumab) |
| **Device/Logo Mark** | Visual symbol or graphic | The green-and-yellow **Monsanto** (now Bayer) logo |
| **Combination Mark** | Word + device combined | "**Pfizer**" word with blue oval logo |
| **Shape Mark** | Three-dimensional product shape | Distinctive inhaler shape (e.g., Seretide® Accuhaler) |
| **Sound Mark** | Distinctive audio signature | The three-note **Pfizer** "boom-boom-boom" jingle (US); heartbeat sound for medical apps |
| **Slogan/Tagline Mark** | Distinctive phrase | "**Cipla** — Caring for Life" |
| **Color Mark** | Single or combination of colors | The **purple** color for Cadbury® chocolate (UK/EU) |
| **Certification Mark** | Indicates quality/origin | "**India Organic**" certification mark |
| **Collective Mark** | Used by a group/association | "**OPPI**" mark for pharma industry body |
| **Series Mark** | Variations of a common mark | "**Genzyme**-1, **Genzyme**-2, **Genzyme**-3" |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Marks that are **inherently distinctive** (fanciful/arbitrary) are given stronger protection than descriptive marks. Biotech companies should file combination marks combining word + logo to maximize cross-jurisdictional protection.

---

### **Question B: Alternative Comprehensive Answer**

**(a)** What is **trademark infringement**? Discuss the statutory remedies available under the Trade Marks Act, 1999, with relevant case laws. [7 Marks]

**(b)** Explain the **Madrid Protocol** for international trademark registration. How does it benefit an Indian biotech startup exporting globally? [7 Marks]

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) Trademark Infringement and Remedies**

**Definition (Section 29, Trade Marks Act, 1999):**
A trademark is infringed when a person, not being the registered proprietor or a permitted user, uses an **identical or deceptively similar mark** in the course of trade in relation to identical or similar goods/services, causing **likelihood of confusion or deception** among the public.

**Tests for Infringement:**
1. **Visual Similarity** — Side-by-side comparison of marks
2. **Phonetic Similarity** — Whether marks sound alike when spoken
3. **Conceptual Similarity** — Similarity of meaning/idea
4. **Similarity of Goods/Services** — Whether they compete in the same market
5. **Likelihood of Confusion** — The ultimate statutory test (Section 29(1))

**Statutory Remedies:**

| Remedy Type | Legal Provision | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Civil — Injunction** | Section 134 | Restraining order (interim/permanent) |
| **Civil — Damages** | Section 135 | Monetary compensation for losses |
| **Civil — Account of Profits** | Section 135 | Infringer surrenders profits earned |
| **Civil — Delivery Up** | Section 135 | Seizure of infringing goods |
| **Criminal — Imprisonment** | Section 103 | 6 months to 3 years |
| **Criminal — Fine** | Section 103 | ₹50,000 to ₹2,00,000 |
| **Border Seizure** | Section 135 | Customs authorities seize counterfeit imports/exports |
| **Anton Piller Order** | English precedent (adopted in India) | Surprise raid on infringer's premises |

**Landmark Case Law:**

- **Cadila Health Care Ltd. v. Cadila Pharmaceuticals Ltd. (2001)** — The Supreme Court laid down the **6-factor test** for determining likelihood of confusion:
  1. Nature of the marks (word, device, composite)
  2. Degree of resemblance
  3. Nature of goods
  4. Class of purchasers
  5. Mode of purchase
  6. Any other surrounding circumstances

- **Satyam Infoway Ltd. v. Sifynet Solutions Pvt. Ltd. (2004)** — Established that **domain names** are protected as trademarks under the Trade Marks Act, 1999.

- **L'Oreal SA v. Bellure NV (2009)** — Extended protection to **comparative advertising** and trade dress.

**(b) Madrid Protocol for International Trademark Registration**

The **Madrid Protocol (1989)** is an international treaty administered by the **World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO)** in Geneva. It provides a **centralized, cost-effective mechanism** for registering trademarks in multiple countries through a single application.

**Key Features:**

- **Single Application:** Filed in one language (English, French, or Spanish) at the applicant's home trademark office.
- **Designated Countries:** The applicant can simultaneously designate **up to 130+ member countries**.
- **Single Fee:** Paid in Swiss Francs (CHF) covering both the international fee and the fees of designated countries.
- **International Registration:** WIPO records the mark in the **International Register** and notifies each designated national office.
- **Substantive Examination:** Each designated country examines the mark under its **own national law** within **12 months** (or longer for some).
- **Centralized Management:** Subsequent changes (renewal, assignment, address change) handled through a single procedural step.

**Benefits for Indian Biotech Startups:**

1. **Cost Efficiency:** Avoids filing 100+ separate national applications. Single-fee structure saves **60–80%** in legal and translation costs.
2. **Speed:** Simultaneous protection in all designated countries within **18–24 months** versus 3–5 years via the Paris Convention national route.
3. **Simplified Renewal:** One renewal application covers all designated countries every 10 years.
4. **Portfolio Centralization:** A single international registration number for cross-border licensing, M&A, and enforcement.
5. **Strategic Market Entry:** Indian biotech startups can secure trademarks in priority export markets (US, EU, Japan, Brazil, South Africa) without engaging local attorneys in each jurisdiction.
6. **Brand Consistency:** Ensures identical brand presentation across global markets — critical for biologics, vaccines, and GM crops.

> [!NOTE]
> **India joined the Madrid Protocol on 8 July 2013.** Indian applicants designate countries through the **International Bureau of WIPO** via the Indian Trade Marks Registry as the office of origin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the Madrid Protocol with the Madrid Agreement (1891).** The Protocol is the more recent, more flexible treaty.
> 2. **Missing the WIPO link** — The International Bureau of WIPO, not the WTO, administers the system.
> 3. **Failing to mention the home office requirement** — Indian applicants can only file through the Indian Trade Marks Registry as the office of origin.
> 4. **Omitting the 12-month national examination deadline.**
> 5. **Calling it a "World Trademark"** — there is no such concept; protection is still territorial.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Trademarks**

- ✔ **Trademark = Brand identifier** governed by the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** in India.
- ✔ **Section 2(zb)** contains the statutory definition; **Section 9** lists absolute grounds for refusal; **Section 11** lists relative grounds.
- ✔ **Registration validity = 10 years**, renewable indefinitely under **Section 25**.
- ✔ **Opposition window = 4 months** post-Journal publication.
- ✔ **45 Nice Classes** — Classes 1–34 (Goods), Classes 35–45 (Services); **Class 5 = Pharma/Biotech**.
- ✔ **™ = Unregistered; ® = Registered.** Misuse of ® is a **criminal offence** under Section 107.
- ✔ **Abercrombie Spectrum** = Generic < Descriptive < Suggestive < Arbitrary < Fanciful (increasing protection).
- ✔ **Infringement = Section 29**; **Criminal Penalty = Section 103** (₹50,000–₹2,00,000 fine + 6 months–3 years imprisonment).
- ✔ **Madrid Protocol (1989)** — Single WIPO-administered international registration route; **India joined 8 July 2013**.
- ✔ **TRIPS Article 16** — Sets the WTO minimum standard for trademark protection globally.
- ✔ **Biotech relevance:** Monoclonal antibodies (mab suffix), GM crops, diagnostic kits, vaccines all rely on strong trademark portfolios for brand equity and investor confidence.
- ✔ **Royalty rate for biologics = 0.5%–10%**; **Trademark value** = Sum of discounted future royalty streams.
- ✔ **Cadila v. Cadila (2001)** — Key Supreme Court precedent on the 6-factor likelihood-of-confusion test.
- ✔ **Convention priority period = 6 months** under the Paris Convention for filing abroad after first domestic application.
- ✔ **Cert marks, Collective marks, Series marks, Sound marks, Color marks, Shape marks** are all recognized in India.

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Copyright (Statutory Definition)**
> Copyright is a **legal intellectual property right** granted by the Copyright Act, 1957 (India) to the **creators of original literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works**, including computer software, sound recordings, and cinematograph films. It confers upon the creator (author) the **exclusive right** to reproduce, distribute, communicate, adapt, and publicly display the work for a specified period, thereby protecting the expression of ideas rather than the ideas themselves.

In the biotechnology entrepreneurship context, copyright is one of the **four pillars of Intellectual Property (IP)**, alongside **Patents, Trademarks, and Trade Secrets**. It specifically protects the **expression** of an idea — not the underlying invention, discovery, or biological process itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Under the topic *Copyrights*, students must understand:
> - Definition, scope, and nature of copyright
> - Subject matter eligible for copyright protection
> - Rights conferred on the copyright holder
> - Duration of protection
> - Infringement and remedies
> - Relevance to biotechnology products, databases, software, and publications

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making Copyright Intuitive

Imagine you write a beautiful **recipe** for a unique herbal formulation. The **recipe written on paper** — the actual text, the diagrams, the layout of your cookbook — is what copyright protects. However, the **act of making the formulation** (i.e., the underlying process) is what a *patent* protects. The **brand name** you sell it under is what a *trademark* protects. Copyright does not cover the smell, the taste, or the chemical activity — only the **written expression**.

> [!TIP]
> **"Copyright protects the *book*, Patent protects the *invention*, Trademark protects the *brand*."**
> This one-line mnemonic resolves 90% of IP confusion in KTU exam answers.

For a biotech startup, this distinction is critical:
- The **research paper** describing a novel enzyme → Copyright protects the article text.
- The **novel enzyme itself** → Patent protects the molecule/process.
- The **company logo / brand name** → Trademark protects identity.
- The **confidential fermentation yield data** → Trade secret protects the formula.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Author** — The natural person who creates the work; the first owner of copyright.
- **Literary Work** — Includes computer programs, tables, compilations, and databases (per the 1994 amendment to the Indian Copyright Act).
- **Artistic Work** — Photographs, paintings, diagrams, maps, and architectural plans.
- **Sound Recording** — A recording of sounds from which sounds can be produced.
- **Cinematograph Film** — Any work of visual recording on any medium.
- **Infringement** — Any act that violates the exclusive rights of the copyright owner without authorization.
- **Fair Use / Fair Dealing** — Statutory exceptions permitting limited use of copyrighted material for purposes such as research, criticism, or education (Section 52 of the Act).
- **Berne Convention** — International treaty governing reciprocal copyright protection across **179+ member countries** (India is a signatory since 1928).
- **WIPO Copyright Treaty (WCT)** — A 1996 special agreement under the Berne Convention that addresses digital environment protections.

---

## 1.4 Why Copyright Matters in Biotechnology Entrepreneurship

A biotech enterprise produces large volumes of copyrightable material:

| Asset | Example | Copyright Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Research publications | Journal articles, review papers | Direct copyright in literary work |
| Database of genetic sequences | Manually curated bioinformatics database | Copyright in compilation (Indian law) |
| Software & algorithms | BLAST, gene prediction tools | Copyright in computer program |
| Promotional content | Website text, brochures, videos | Copyright in artistic/literary work |
| Training material | SOPs, manuals, lab protocols | Copyright in literary work |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do **not** confuse copyright registration with copyright existence. In India, **copyright exists the moment a work is created and fixed in a tangible medium** — registration is only **prima facie evidence** in a court of law, not a precondition for protection.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Analysis Sheet

## 2.1 Subject Matter of Copyright (Section 13 of the Copyright Act, 1957)

Copyright protection subsists throughout India in the following classes of works:

1. **Original literary works** — books, articles, computer programs, tables, compilations (including databases).
2. **Original dramatic works** — scripts, choreographic works (not cinematographic).
3. **Original musical works** — compositions, with or without words.
4. **Original artistic works** — paintings, drawings, photographs, sculptures, architectural plans.
5. **Cinematograph films** — visual recordings with accompanying sound.
6. **Sound recordings** — phonographic records, digital audio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024 ESE — remember the sixfold classification.** A common question asks: *"Is a computer program eligible for copyright?"* — the answer is **YES, as a literary work** under the 1994 amendment.

---

## 2.2 The 'Idea–Expression Dichotomy' (Foundational Principle)

This is the **single most important concept** in copyright law, especially relevant for biotechnology:

> **Copyright protects the *expression* of an idea, NOT the idea itself.**

In biotech terms:
- The **idea** of using CRISPR to edit a specific gene → **Not protected** by copyright (may be patentable).
- The **written description** of that idea in a research paper → **Protected** by copyright.
- The **actual gene sequence** (as information) → **Not protected** by copyright (may be patentable subject matter).
- A **curated genomic database** with original arrangement/selection → **Protected** as a compilation.

---

## 2.3 Rights Conferred on the Copyright Owner (Section 14)

The copyright owner has **six exclusive economic rights**:

| Right | Meaning | Biotech Example |
|---|---|---|
| Right to reproduce | Make copies of the work | Photocopying a lab manual |
| Right to communicate to public | Broadcast/internet dissemination | Uploading SOPs to YouTube |
| Right to issue copies | First distribution of copies | Selling published research |
| Right to adapt/translate | Create derivative works | Translating a textbook |
| Right to perform publicly | Stage, recite, or play the work | Presenting a research lecture |
| Right to cinematograph | Record the work visually | Filming a research demonstration |

Additionally, the author has **moral rights** (Section 57):
- Right to claim authorship (paternity right).
- Right to integrity — to prevent distortion, mutilation, or modification prejudicial to honor or reputation.

---

## 2.4 Duration of Copyright (Section 22–29)

| Work Type | Duration |
|---|---|
| Literary, dramatic, musical, artistic works | **Life of the author + 60 years** |
| Anonymous or pseudonymous works | **60 years from the date of publication** |
| Cinematograph films & sound recordings | **60 years from the date of publication** |
| Government works | **60 years from the date of publication** |
| Works of international organizations | **60 years from the date of publication** |

> [!TIP]
> **After expiry, the work enters the public domain** and can be freely used by anyone.

---

## 2.5 Ownership and Assignment

- **First owner** = the author (Section 17).
- **Employee created work during employment** → employer owns it (in the absence of contrary agreement), *only if* the work was created in the course of employment using employer's resources.
- **Commissioned work** (e.g., a consultant writing a report) → the **commissioner** owns the copyright, unless otherwise agreed.

For a biotech startup:
- A **research associate** developing software as part of their job → copyright vests in the **company**, not the individual.
- A **freelance scientific illustrator** hired for a paper → copyright vests in the **author/illustrator**, not the commissioning institution, unless assigned.

---

## 2.6 Copyright Infringement (Section 51)

Infringement occurs when a person, without a license from the owner:
- Does anything the copyright owner has the exclusive right to do.
- Permits for profit any place to be used for communication of the work to the public.
- Imports infringing copies.

### 2.6.1 Civil Remedies (Section 55–58)
- **Injunction** (temporary or permanent).
- **Damages** (compensatory and punitive).
- **Account of profits**.
- **Order for delivery** of infringing copies.

### 2.6.2 Criminal Remedies (Section 62–64)
- Imprisonment up to **3 years**, OR
- Fine up to **₹2,00,000**, OR
- Both.
- For subsequent offences: minimum **1 year imprisonment** and fine of **₹50,000 minimum**.

### 2.6.3 Statutory Exceptions — Fair Dealing (Section 52)

Permitted uses (highly relevant in academic biotech research):
- Private or personal use, including research.
- Criticism or review.
- Reporting of current events.
- Use in judicial proceedings.
- Quotation for legitimate purposes.
- Use in education (in institutions, not for commercial training).

---

## 2.7 Copyright Registration Procedure in India

While not mandatory, registration confers evidentiary advantages:

| Step | Authority | Time Frame |
|---|---|---|
| 1. File Form XIV (with prescribed fee) | Copyright Office, New Delhi | Day 0 |
| 2. Examination of application | Registrar of Copyrights | 30 days |
| 3. Objection period (if any) | Public notice in newspapers | 30 days |
| 4. Registration granted | Entry in Register of Copyrights | 6–12 months total |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Edge:** A question often asks — *Is copyright registration compulsory?* **No**, but it is **admissible as prima facie evidence** in court. For biotech publications and software, this is a **highly recommended** defensive measure.

---

## 2.8 International Copyright Framework

| Treaty | Year | Key Provision |
|---|---|---|
| Berne Convention | 1886 | Automatic protection without formalities; national treatment |
| Universal Copyright Convention | 1952 | Alternative to Berne; symbol © |
| TRIPS Agreement | 1994 | WTO members must protect computer programs as literary works |
| WIPO Copyright Treaty | 1996 | Digital environment; anti-circumvention |
| Marrakesh Treaty | 2013 | Access to published works for visually impaired |

> **For biotech startups exporting products, services, or content** — international treaties ensure that Indian copyright is **automatically recognized in 179+ Berne member countries**.

---

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Analytical Summary

| Concept | Exam-Ready One-Liner |
|---|---|
| Copyright protects | **Expression**, not idea |
| Duration for literary work | **Life of author + 60 years** |
| First owner | **Author** (with statutory exceptions) |
| Computer program | **Literary work** (since 1994) |
| Registration | **Not compulsory**, but advisable |
| Moral rights | **Perpetual, inalienable** (Section 57) |
| Criminal penalty | Up to **3 years imprisonment + ₹2 lakh fine** |
| Indian statute | **Copyright Act, 1957** (as amended in 2012) |
| International treaty | **Berne Convention** |
| Idea–Expression dichotomy | **The cornerstone of copyright** |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step: How to Identify Whether a Biotech Asset Qualifies for Copyright

Biotech entrepreneurs often face the dilemma: *"Is this asset mine, and how do I protect it?"* The following is the **analytical decision framework** a KTU student must follow:

### Step 1: Identify the Work

Ask: *Is the asset a tangible expression fixed in a medium?*
- A research paper, software code, database, photograph, lab protocol manual, SOP, training video, scientific illustration.

### Step 2: Verify Originality

Ask: *Was the work created through the author's own skill, judgment, and effort?*
- In India, **sweat of the brow doctrine** (substantial effort) is recognized for compilations/databases.
- A mere photocopy of another work → **No copyright**.

### Step 3: Check the Idea–Expression Dichotomy

Ask: *Am I claiming copyright over the idea, or over its expression?*
- If idea → **Not copyrightable** (may be patentable / trade secret).
- If expression → **Copyrightable**.

### Step 4: Determine the Author and First Owner

Apply Section 17:
- Was the work created by an employee in the course of employment? → **Employer is owner**.
- Was it commissioned? → **Commissioner is owner** (unless contract states otherwise).
- Otherwise → **The author is the first owner**.

### Step 5: Ascertain Duration

Use Section 22–29:
- Literary/Artistic/Dramatic/Musical → **Life + 60 years**.
- Films/Sound Recordings → **60 years from publication**.

### Step 6: Apply Fair Dealing Test (if challenged)

Ask: *Does the use fall under Section 52 exceptions?*
- Private research? Criticism? Educational use in non-profit institutions?
- If yes → **Not infringement**.

### Step 7: Decide on Registration

If the work is commercially significant:
- File Form XIV at the **Copyright Office, New Delhi** under the Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT).
- Pay prescribed fee, wait for examination, publish in newspaper for objections, receive registration.

### Step 8: Maintain Records of Creation

- Save drafts, version history, email trails, git commits.
- These serve as **prior art and authorship evidence** in case of disputes.

---

## 3.2 Comparative Decision Matrix: Copyright vs. Other IP Rights

A KTU examiner frequently tests whether the student can **distinguish copyright from patent/trademark/trade secret**. Use this analytical matrix:

| Criterion | Copyright | Patent | Trademark | Trade Secret |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Protects** | Expression of idea | Invention (novel, useful) | Brand identifier | Confidential information |
| **Subject** | Literary, artistic, software | Process, product, composition | Logo, name, slogan | Formula, process, customer list |
| **Acquisition** | Automatic on creation | After grant (application) | After registration | Through confidentiality measures |
| **Duration** | Life + 60 yrs | 20 years from filing | 10 years (renewable) | Indefinite (till secret kept) |
| **Registration** | Optional (recommended) | Mandatory | Mandatory | No registration system |
| **Biotech Example** | Research article | Recombinant DNA | "Biocon" logo | Fermentation yield data |
| **Disclosure Required** | No | Yes (full disclosure) | Yes (logo, classes) | No |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Case Walkthrough — KTU Pattern

> **Case:** A biotech company *Genomika Pvt. Ltd.* develops a novel bioinformatics tool called *SeqAlign Pro*. The lead scientist, Dr. A, writes 50,000 lines of original code. Dr. A then publishes a paper in *Nature Biotechnology* describing the algorithm. The company logo is "G-Seq." A competitor copies the code into their own product.

### Step 1: Is SeqAlign Pro code copyrightable?
Yes, as a **literary work** (computer program) under Section 2(o) of the Copyright Act.

### Step 2: Who owns the copyright?
Under Section 17, since the code was developed by Dr. A **in the course of employment** using company resources, the **company Genomika Pvt. Ltd. is the first owner**, not Dr. A personally.

### Step 3: What about the Nature Biotechnology paper?
The **publishing journal often holds the copyright** (via transfer agreement). The author retains **moral rights** (paternity + integrity) under Section 57.

### Step 4: What about the logo "G-Seq"?
This is **not copyrightable** as such (logos are typically registered under trademark). However, its **artistic design** may have thin copyright protection.

### Step 5: Infringement by competitor
The competitor copying the code is **copyright infringement**. Genomika can seek:
- **Civil remedies**: Injunction, damages, account of profits.
- **Criminal remedies**: Imprisonment up to 3 years, fine up to ₹2 lakh.

### Step 6: Defence of the competitor?
The competitor may argue **fair dealing under Section 52**, but commercial copying for a competing product is **highly unlikely to qualify**.

---

## 3.4 Algorithmic Decision Tree — Mermaid Representation

> Note: Since this section is intended for analytical understanding, a **flowchart of the decision logic** is provided in Section 4 alongside other structural diagrams.

---

## 3.5 Numerical / Formula-Style Treatment

While copyright law does not use mathematical formulas, the **valuation of copyright** in a biotech company is often required in business contexts. A simple **capitalized royalty income model** can be expressed as:

$$
V_{c} \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{R_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}}
$$

Where:
- $V_{c}$ = Present value of the copyright asset.
- $R_{t}$ = Royalty income in year $t$.
- $i$ = Discount rate.
- $n$ = Remaining economic life of the copyright.

> **Note for KTU students:** This is the **Income Approach** to valuing intangible assets, often used in IP due-diligence and startup valuation. While not the core of copyright theory, it is a **high-value conceptual tool** for an entrepreneurship-focused syllabus.

The income $R_{t}$ can further be expressed as:

$$
R_{t} \;=\; S_{t} \times r
$$

Where $S_{t}$ is the sales revenue in year $t$ and $r$ is the negotiated royalty rate (typically **2% to 10%** for biotech products).
